blocks.text: Linux filesystem is a single tree rooted at /. Everything is a file — regular files, directories, devices, sockets. Key directories: /etc (system config files), /var (variable data: logs, spool, databases), /home (user home dirs), /tmp (temp files, world-writable), /proc (virtual filesystem exposing kernel/process info), /dev (device files), /usr (user programs and libraries), /bin and /usr/bin (executable commands), /lib and /usr/lib (shared libraries).

pwd           # print working directory
cd /path      # change directory (absolute)
cd ../..      # change directory (relative, up 2 levels)
ls -la        # list all files including hidden, long format
tree -L 2     # show directory tree 2 levels deep
realpath file # show absolute path
ln -s target link  # create symbolic link

qas.q: What is /proc in Linux?
qas.a: /proc is a virtual filesystem generated by the kernel on-the-fly — no actual disk files. It exposes kernel and process information. /proc/PID/ contains info about each running process (fd/, maps, status, cmdline). /proc/cpuinfo, /proc/meminfo expose hardware info. Tools like top and ps read from /proc. Reading a /proc file calls a kernel function that generates the content dynamically.

qas.q: What is the difference between an absolute and relative path?
qas.a: Absolute path: starts from the root (/). Always the same regardless of current directory. Example: /home/user/docs/file.txt. Relative path: relative to the current working directory. Example: docs/file.txt (from /home/user). Special entries: . = current directory, .. = parent directory. Use absolute paths in scripts for reliability.

qas.q: What is the purpose of /etc vs /var?
qas.a: /etc contains static configuration files that rarely change — system init files, network config, cron jobs, user/group definitions. /var contains variable data that changes during runtime — log files, mail spools, database files, PID files, package caches. Conceptually: /etc is what you configure, /var is what the system writes at runtime.

blocks.text: Everyday file operations are tested in interviews through 'how would you do X' scenarios. Know the key commands cold: copy (cp), move/rename (mv), delete (rm, rm -rf for recursive), create directory (mkdir -p for nested), view files (cat for small, less for paging, head/tail for first/last lines, tail -f for live log following).

cp -r src/ dst/          # copy directory recursively
mv file.txt newname.txt  # rename/move
rm -rf dir/              # delete directory (DANGEROUS — no undo)
mkdir -p a/b/c           # create nested directories
cat file.txt             # dump file to stdout
less file.txt            # page through file (q to quit)
tail -f /var/log/syslog  # follow log file in real time
find . -name '*.log'     # find files by name pattern
find . -mtime -1         # files modified in last 24 hours
stat file.txt            # file metadata (size, times, inode)

qas.q: What is the difference between cp and mv at the filesystem level?
qas.a: cp: creates a new file (new inode), reads the source data, writes to destination. Source remains. Works across filesystems. mv: if same filesystem, just updates directory entries (rename the path — O(1), no data copied). If across filesystems, copies data then deletes source. This is why mv on the same disk is instant but mv to a different disk takes time.

qas.q: How would you find all .log files modified in the last 24 hours?
qas.a: find /var/log -name '*.log' -mtime -1. The -mtime -1 means modification time less than 1 day ago. For minutes: -mmin -60. You can also combine conditions: -name '*.log' -size +1M (larger than 1MB) using -and. To execute a command on each: -exec grep 'ERROR' {} +

qas.q: How does tail -f work?
qas.a: tail -f watches the file descriptor and outputs new bytes as they're appended. Useful for monitoring live log files. If the log is rotated (replaced with new file), use tail -F instead — it follows the filename, reopening when the file is replaced. In modern systems, journalctl -f follows the systemd journal.

blocks.text: Every file/directory has a 9-bit permission: rwx (read, write, execute) for owner, group, and others. r=4, w=2, x=1 — sum them for octal notation. 755 = rwxr-xr-x (owner all, group and others can read and execute). 644 = rw-r--r-- (owner rw, others read-only). For directories: x means you can enter it (cd), r means you can list contents (ls), w means you can create/delete files inside.

ls -la          # shows permissions: -rwxr-xr-x 1 user group 1234
chmod 755 file  # set permissions numerically
chmod +x file   # add execute for all
chmod u-w file  # remove write from owner
chown user file # change owner
chown user:group file  # change owner and group
id              # show current user's uid, gid, groups
umask           # show default permission mask

qas.q: What does chmod 755 mean?
qas.a: Owner gets 7=rwx (read, write, execute). Group gets 5=r-x (read, execute, no write). Others get 5=r-x. Typical for web server files/scripts — owner can edit, everyone can execute. chmod 644 = owner rw, others read-only — typical for config files. chmod 600 = owner rw only — sensitive files (SSH private keys).

qas.q: What is the setuid bit and why is it a security risk?
qas.a: When setuid is set on an executable, it runs with the owner's privileges instead of the calling user's. /usr/bin/passwd has setuid root — a normal user can run it to change their own password because it needs root to write /etc/shadow. Security risk: if a setuid-root program has a vulnerability (buffer overflow), an attacker can exploit it to get root shell. Always audit setuid programs.

qas.q: What is the sticky bit on a directory?
qas.a: On directories, sticky bit means only the file's owner (and root) can delete or rename files in that directory, even if others have write permission on the directory. /tmp has sticky bit set (drwxrwxrwt) — everyone can create files, but you can't delete other users' files. Without sticky bit on a shared directory, anyone with write on the dir could delete anyone's files.

qas.q: What is umask?
qas.a: umask is a mask subtracted from the default permissions when files/directories are created. Default permissions: 666 for files, 777 for dirs. With umask 022: files get 666-022=644, dirs get 777-022=755. With umask 027: files get 640, dirs get 750 (group can read, others nothing). Check with 'umask'; set in ~/.bashrc or /etc/profile.

blocks.text: Unix philosophy: small tools that do one thing well, composable via pipes. Pipes (|) connect stdout of one command to stdin of the next. Redirection: > (overwrite file), >> (append), < (read from file), 2> (redirect stderr), 2>&1 (merge stderr into stdout). Master grep, awk, sed, sort, uniq, wc, cut — these appear in production debugging and interviews.

# Find lines with 'ERROR' in log, count occurrences
grep -c 'ERROR' app.log

# Show lines 10 before and after each ERROR
grep -A10 -B10 'ERROR' app.log

# Count unique IPs in access log
awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

# Replace 'localhost' with '127.0.0.1' in file (in-place)
sed -i 's/localhost/127.0.0.1/g' config.txt

# Print lines 5-10 of a file
sed -n '5,10p' file.txt

# Count lines/words/chars in file
wc -l file.txt

qas.q: How would you find the top 5 most frequent IP addresses in an nginx access log?
qas.a: awk '{print $1}' /var/log/nginx/access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -5. Breakdown: awk prints first field (IP), sort groups IPs together, uniq -c counts consecutive identical lines, sort -rn sorts numerically descending, head -5 takes top 5.

qas.q: What is the difference between > and >>?
qas.a: > overwrites the file (creates new if doesn't exist, truncates if does). >> appends to the file (creates new if doesn't exist, adds to end if does). Example: echo 'line' > file (replaces content), echo 'line' >> file (adds to end). Be careful with > — it truncates even before the command runs, so a failed command can empty your file.

qas.q: How would you extract only the third column from a CSV file?
qas.a: cut -d',' -f3 file.csv. -d specifies delimiter, -f specifies field number. For more complex processing: awk -F',' '{print $3}' file.csv. awk is more powerful for conditional logic or multiple columns. For TSV (tab-separated): cut -f3 file.tsv (tab is default delimiter).

qas.q: What does 2>&1 mean?
qas.a: Redirects stderr (file descriptor 2) to wherever stdout (file descriptor 1) is currently pointing. command > output.log 2>&1 sends both stdout and stderr to output.log. Order matters: command 2>&1 > file redirects stderr to the original stdout (terminal), then redirects stdout to file — wrong if you want both in file. Use command > file 2>&1.

blocks.text: Every process has a PID (process ID) and PPID (parent process ID). Processes form a tree rooted at PID 1 (init/systemd). The kernel scheduler assigns CPU time. Process states in Linux: Running (R), Sleeping interruptible (S), Sleeping uninterruptible (D — waiting for I/O, can't be killed), Zombie (Z), Stopped (T). A high number of processes in D state often indicates an I/O or NFS problem.

ps aux             # list all processes
ps -ef --forest    # process tree
top                # live process monitor
htop               # interactive process monitor
kill PID           # send SIGTERM (graceful)
kill -9 PID        # send SIGKILL (force)
kill -l            # list all signals
nice -n 10 cmd     # run command with lower priority
renice 10 -p PID   # change priority of running process
lsof -p PID        # list files opened by process
lsof -i :8080      # what process is on port 8080

qas.q: What is the difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL?
qas.a: SIGTERM (15): politely request the process to terminate. The process can catch it, flush buffers, release locks, and exit gracefully. Always try this first. SIGKILL (9): kernel immediately destroys the process — no signal handler runs. Used when SIGTERM doesn't work. A process in D state (uninterruptible sleep) can't even be killed by SIGKILL — it must complete its I/O operation first.

qas.q: What is a zombie process?
qas.a: A process that has finished execution but still has an entry in the process table because its parent hasn't called wait() to read its exit code. Shown as Z in ps. Zombies don't consume CPU or memory but do consume a PID slot. Fix: kill the parent process (the init/systemd will adopt orphaned children and call wait()). If the parent is a live server creating zombies, it has a bug — it should call waitpid() on finished child processes.

qas.q: How do you run a long-running command that survives session logout?
qas.a: nohup command & disown — nohup ignores SIGHUP (sent on logout), & runs in background, disown removes it from shell's job list. Alternatively: use tmux or screen (create a persistent terminal session). For production services: use systemd (systemctl start/enable myservice) — proper daemon management with logging, restart-on-failure, and boot startup.

qas.q: What does the nice value do?
qas.a: Controls CPU scheduling priority. Range: -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest). Default is 0. Higher nice value = more 'nice' to other processes = lower CPU priority. Regular users can only increase nice (lower priority). Only root can decrease nice (increase priority). Use nice for CPU-intensive background tasks that shouldn't impact foreground work.

blocks.text: Git is a distributed VCS — every clone has the full history. Core concepts: repository (project + full history), commit (snapshot of all files at a point), branch (movable pointer to a commit), HEAD (pointer to current commit/branch), staging area/index (files prepared for the next commit), remote (shared repository on a server).

git init                    # create new repo
git add file / git add .    # stage changes
git commit -m 'message'     # create snapshot
git status                  # show working tree state
git log --oneline --graph   # compact commit history
git diff                    # unstaged changes
git diff --staged           # staged changes
git branch feature          # create branch
git checkout -b feature     # create + switch
git merge feature           # merge into current branch
git rebase main             # rebase current branch on main
git stash / git stash pop   # save/restore uncommitted work
git reset --soft HEAD~1     # undo last commit, keep staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1     # undo last commit, discard changes
git cherry-pick abc123      # apply specific commit

qas.q: What is the difference between merge and rebase?
qas.a: Merge: creates a merge commit joining two branch histories. Preserves full history, shows when branches diverged and merged. Non-destructive. Rebase: moves your branch's commits on top of the target branch, rewriting commit hashes. Creates a linear history — cleaner but rewrites history. Golden rule: never rebase public/shared branches (confuses other devs). Use rebase for local cleanup; merge for shared branch integration.

qas.q: What is git reset --soft vs --mixed vs --hard?
qas.a: --soft HEAD~1: undo the last commit, keep changes staged (ready to recommit). --mixed HEAD~1 (default): undo the commit and unstage changes, but keep files modified. --hard HEAD~1: undo the commit AND discard all file changes — the files revert to their state at HEAD~1. --hard is destructive. git reflog can help recover lost commits after --hard.

qas.q: What does HEAD mean in Git?
qas.a: HEAD is a pointer to the currently checked-out commit — usually points to the tip of the current branch. HEAD → branch → commit. Detached HEAD: HEAD points directly to a commit, not a branch. Happens after git checkout <commit-hash>. Any commits in detached HEAD state are at risk of being garbage collected unless you create a branch to hold them.

qas.q: What is git stash?
qas.a: git stash saves your uncommitted changes (staged and unstaged) to a stack and reverts your working directory to HEAD. Useful when you need to quickly switch branches without committing half-done work. git stash pop restores the most recent stash. git stash list shows all stashes. git stash apply stash@{2} applies a specific stash without removing it.

blocks.text: SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle) defines phases for software creation: Requirements → System Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance. Waterfall: sequential, each phase complete before next, heavy documentation, hard to change requirements mid-project. Agile: iterative short cycles (sprints, typically 1-2 weeks), frequent delivery, embrace changing requirements, continuous feedback.

Scrum (most common Agile framework): sprint (1-2 week iteration), sprint planning (team commits to user stories), daily standup (what did I do, what will I do, blockers), sprint review (demo to stakeholders), retrospective (improve process). Artifacts: product backlog (all work), sprint backlog (work for current sprint), increment (working software). Roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team. CI/CD: Continuous Integration (merge code frequently, run tests automatically), Continuous Deployment (every passing build deploys to production).

Agile values (Agile Manifesto): Individuals over processes. Working software over documentation. Customer collaboration over contract negotiation. Responding to change over following a plan. Not 'no process' — just 'less ceremony, more results.' Velocity = story points completed per sprint. Used for sprint planning forecasting.

qas.q: What is the difference between Agile and Waterfall?
qas.a: Waterfall: linear phases, requirements frozen upfront, testing at end, high documentation, bad at handling changing requirements. One delivery at project end. Agile: iterative sprints, requirements evolve, continuous testing, frequent small deliveries, teams self-organize. Waterfall suits: fixed requirements, safety-critical systems (embedded, medical). Agile suits: product development, changing requirements, fast feedback.

qas.q: What is CI/CD?
qas.a: CI (Continuous Integration): developers merge code to main branch frequently. Every merge triggers automated build and tests. Catches integration bugs early — no 'merge hell.' CD (Continuous Delivery): every CI-passing build is deployable to production with one click. Continuous Deployment: go further — automatically deploy every passing build. Requires: good test coverage, automated deployment, feature flags, rollback capability.

qas.q: What is a user story?
qas.a: A user story describes a feature from the user's perspective: 'As a [user type], I want [goal] so that [reason].' Example: 'As a registered user, I want to reset my password so I can regain access if I forget it.' Stories have acceptance criteria (conditions that must be true to consider it done). Story points estimate relative effort, not hours. Product Owner writes and prioritizes stories.

blocks.text: Testing levels from narrow to broad: Unit testing (test a single function/method in isolation — fast, no dependencies), Integration testing (test multiple modules working together — DB, API calls), System testing (test the complete application end-to-end), Acceptance testing (does it meet business requirements — UAT). TDD (Test-Driven Development): write the test first, see it fail, write code to pass, refactor.

Mocking: replace real dependencies (database, external API, file system) with controlled fake implementations during tests. Allows unit testing without infrastructure. Stub: returns canned responses. Mock: additionally verifies the expected calls were made. Spy: wraps real implementation, records calls. Code coverage: % of code lines executed by tests. High coverage doesn't guarantee correctness, but low coverage guarantees untested code paths.

Testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap), fewer integration tests (slower), fewer end-to-end tests (slowest, most brittle). Inverted pyramid (too many E2E) = slow, flaky test suite. Stub=return fake data. Mock=verify behavior. Spy=watch real object. TDD cycle: Red (write failing test) → Green (make it pass) → Refactor.

qas.q: What is the difference between unit and integration testing?
qas.a: Unit test: tests a single function or class in isolation. All dependencies are mocked/stubbed. Fast (milliseconds), no I/O. Tells you a piece of code works in isolation. Integration test: tests multiple components together — your code + real database, or your code + real API. Slower, needs infrastructure, but tests that components work together correctly. Both are necessary — unit tests don't catch interface mismatches.

qas.q: What is mocking and why is it useful?
qas.a: Mocking replaces a real dependency with a controlled fake. Benefits: tests run without real infrastructure (no database setup), tests run fast, can simulate error conditions that are hard to reproduce (network timeout, DB failure), isolates the code under test. Downside: mocks can diverge from real behavior — integration tests catch these gaps. Mock the boundary, not the internals.

qas.q: What are the benefits of TDD?
qas.a: Testable design by default — if code is hard to unit test, it's usually poorly designed (too many dependencies). Clear requirements — tests document expected behavior. Refactoring confidence — tests catch regressions. No dead code — you only write code to pass a test. But TDD has overhead: tests take time to write, and changing requirements mean changing tests. Most effective for complex business logic.

blocks.text: Big-O notation describes how runtime or space grows as input size n increases, ignoring constants and lower-order terms. It measures worst-case asymptotic behavior. Complexity hierarchy from best to worst: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2^n) < O(n!). A good algorithm avoids O(n²) for large inputs — it may be 1000x slower than O(n log n) at n=1000.

Common examples: Hash map lookup = O(1). Binary search = O(log n). Traversing an array = O(n). Merge sort/quicksort average = O(n log n). Bubble sort, nested loops = O(n²). Recursive Fibonacci without memoization = O(2^n). Amortized analysis: dynamic array append is O(1) amortized even though occasional resizes are O(n), because the total cost of n appends is O(n).

Drop constants and lower-order terms: O(2n + 5) = O(n). O(n² + n) = O(n²). For nested loops: multiply complexities. For sequential blocks: add (then take the dominant term). Recursion: solve with Master Theorem or draw the recursion tree. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n) (Merge Sort).

qas.q: What is the difference between Big-O, Big-Theta, and Big-Omega?
qas.a: Big-O: upper bound (worst case — 'at most this fast'). Big-Omega: lower bound (best case — 'at least this fast'). Big-Theta: tight bound (average case — 'exactly this fast asymptotically'). Interviews almost always mean Big-O when they say 'what's the complexity.' For binary search: best case O(1) (target is middle), worst case O(log n), average O(log n).

qas.q: What is amortized analysis?
qas.a: Amortized analysis gives the average cost per operation over a sequence of operations, even if individual operations vary. Classic example: dynamic array (ArrayList). Normally O(1) append. When full, doubles capacity and copies all elements — O(n). Amortized: doubling happens at sizes 1, 2, 4, 8... Total copy work for n elements = 1+2+4+...+n = 2n. Average per append = 2n/n = O(1) amortized.

qas.q: How do you analyze the time complexity of a recursive algorithm?
qas.a: Write the recurrence relation. Apply Master Theorem for divide-and-conquer: T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^c). If a < b^c: O(n^c). If a = b^c: O(n^c log n). If a > b^c: O(n^log_b(a)). Merge sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → a=2, b=2, c=1 → a=b^c → O(n log n). Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → O(log n).

qas.q: What is the space complexity of a recursive function?
qas.a: Each recursive call adds a stack frame — space complexity = O(recursion depth). Fibonacci recursive: depth n → O(n) space. Binary search recursive: depth log n → O(log n) space. Can be O(1) if tail-recursive and language supports tail-call optimization. DFS on a tree: O(h) space where h is tree height. This is why iterative solutions using explicit stacks can be better for deep recursions.

blocks.text: System design interviews test your ability to design scalable, reliable systems. Core concepts: scalability (handle more load), availability (stay up), consistency (correct data), latency (response time). Vertical scaling: bigger machine (CPU, RAM). Horizontal scaling: more machines behind a load balancer. Stateless services scale horizontally easily; stateful services (DBs) are harder.

CAP Theorem: in a distributed system, you can guarantee at most two of: Consistency (every read gets the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition Tolerance (system works despite network partitions). Since partitions are unavoidable in real networks, you choose CP (consistent, may be unavailable during partition — ZooKeeper, HBase) or AP (available, may return stale data — Cassandra, DynamoDB). SQL databases are CA in single-node but CP in distributed.

Load balancer distributes traffic (Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash). Cache layers: CDN (static assets), Application cache (Redis/Memcached for DB query results), DB cache (query cache, buffer pool). Cache invalidation strategies: TTL-based, write-through (update cache on write), write-around (write to DB, invalidate cache), write-back (write to cache, async to DB — risk of data loss).

qas.q: Explain CAP theorem.
qas.a: CAP: Consistency (all nodes return same data), Availability (every request gets a non-error response), Partition Tolerance (works during network splits). You can't have all three in a distributed system. Partitions happen in real networks, so the real choice is CP vs AP. CP: favor consistency over availability (bank transactions). AP: favor availability over consistency (social media likes, shopping carts). Many modern systems allow tunable consistency.

qas.q: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL?
qas.a: SQL (relational): structured schema, ACID transactions, powerful joins, scales vertically well, horizontal sharding is hard. Best for: complex queries, relationships, transactions (banking, ERP). NoSQL: flexible schema (document, key-value, column, graph), designed for horizontal scaling, eventual consistency by default, limited join capability. Best for: high write throughput, simple access patterns, hierarchical data, huge scale (Cassandra, MongoDB, Redis).

qas.q: What are the trade-offs of adding a cache?
qas.a: Benefits: reduced DB load, lower latency, higher throughput. Trade-offs: cache invalidation complexity (stale data), memory cost, cache stampede (many requests miss at once and hammer the DB), cold start (empty cache on deploy). Not worth it for: write-heavy data, frequently changing data, small datasets that fit in DB memory anyway. Always profile before caching.

qas.q: How would you design a URL shortener?
qas.a: Core: store mapping of shortCode → longURL in a DB (Redis for speed + DB for persistence). Short code: base62 encoding of auto-increment ID (or MD5/SHA hash truncated). API: POST /shorten (create), GET /:code (redirect with 301/302). Scale: read-heavy, so add read replicas and cache hot short codes in Redis. Avoid hash collisions by checking existence before storing. Rate limit creation API.
